coredata.dc:title: In situ coupling of carbon dots with zeolitic imidazolate frameworks enabling highly red emission in solid state
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Wen
Wei, Jinbao
Ding, Yutong
Yang, Yike
Zhou, Xin
Meng, Hong-min
Zhu, Qianqian
Li, Zhaohui
coredata.dc:description: In this work, an extraordinary solid red emissive phosphor was prepared based on red-emitting carbon dots (R-CDs). The synthesis was conducted under an in-situ strategy, with assistance of zeolitic imidazolate frameworks. The obtained phosphor possesses a stronger red emission located at 630 nm in solid state, with CIE coordinate of (0.63, 0.35) and quantum yield of ∼ 45 %. As a consequence, not only aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching of R-CDs is avoided in solid state, but also an enhanced emission with high quantum yield is presented. Fluorescence properties were further explored in detail. The emission is found to be responsive to temperature and applied pressure. Based on the excellent emissive performance, the material has great potentials in anti-counterfeiting, latent fingerprint imaging, and temperature/pressure-sensing. This work provides a facile and promising way of preparing solid carbon-based phosphors for special applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Long-wavelength emission
Solid-state fluorescence
Temperature
Pressure

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural characterizations: SEM and TEM photographs of SR-CDs (a, b), TEM photograph of R-CDs with the inset of size distribution (c), and PXRD diffraction patterns (d).
XPS spectrum of SR-CDs (a); FTIR absorption spectra of R-CDs, ZIF-8, and SR-CDs (b).

Fluorescence properties of SR-CDs: emission spectra under different excitation wavelengths (a), the emission stability under continuous illumination (b), and time-resolved fluorescence spectrum monitored at 630 nm (c).

Variable-temperature fluorescence properties of SR-CDs: variable-temperature fluorescence spectra (a), the maximum emission intensity as a function of temperature (b), the relationship between the integrated emission intensity and temperature, with the fitting curve based on the reported equation [31] (c), CIE color plot related to the variable-temperature fluorescence spectra (d), variable-temperature fluorescence decays (e), and the plot of calculated lifetime values versus temperature (f).
Emission spectra (a), the CIE color plot (b), fluorescence quantum yields (c) and fluorescence decay curves (d) of SR-CDs under different applied pressure.

head.ce:title: In situ coupling of carbon dots with zeolitic imidazolate frameworks enabling highly red emission in solid state

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Institute of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and Their Bioanalytical Applications, Zhengzhou Key Laboratory of Functional Nanomaterial and Medical Theranostic, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An extraordinary red emissive phosphor was reported based on red-emitting carbon dots (R-CDs) through an in-situ encapsulation strategy. The obtained nanocomposite possesses a bright red emission at solid state, with CIE coordinate of (0.63, 0.35) and quantum yield of ∼ 45%.

In this work, an extraordinary solid red emissive phosphor was prepared based on red-emitting carbon dots (R-CDs). The synthesis was conducted under an in-situ strategy, with assistance of zeolitic imidazolate frameworks. The obtained phosphor possesses a stronger red emission located at 630 nm in solid state, with CIE coordinate of (0.63, 0.35) and quantum yield of ∼ 45 %. As a consequence, not only aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching of R-CDs is avoided in solid state, but also an enhanced emission with high quantum yield is presented. Fluorescence properties were further explored in detail. The emission is found to be responsive to temperature and applied pressure. Based on the excellent emissive performance, the material has great potentials in anti-counterfeiting, latent fingerprint imaging, and temperature/pressure-sensing. This work provides a facile and promising way of preparing solid carbon-based phosphors for special applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) have been considered as promising nanomaterials. They have drawn great attention, attributed to their distinctive optical properties, nontoxicity, low photo-bleaching, and high photostability [1–4]. They offer great potential for wide applications in bioimaging [5–7], light-emitting diodes [8,9], sensors [10,11], forensic science [12,13], and information encrypting [14,15]. Note that, these applications rely greatly on the photoluminescent properties of CDs in solution or in solid state. However, most of the CDs have been reported and used in the solution form [4,16–20]. CDs are usually subjected to aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching (ACQ), thereby making the facile synthesis and application of solid CDs materials a great challenge [18,21].

To date, diverse synthetic strategies have been applied in the construction of CDs-based solid materials [22–24]. Yan and coworkers synthesized a CDs-based nanocomposite with a strong violet-blue emission by employing montmorillonite as the carrier [23]. Peng et al introduced poly(vinylpyrrolidone) as a cross-linking agent in the fabrication of multicolor emissive CDs powders [24]. Besides, inorganic shell materials, metal–organic frameworks, commercial silica gel and starch are also very popular in the synthesis of solid CDs materials [12,22,25–27]. However, most of the reported solid CDs materials require time-consuming treatments and the yield is very low, making practical applications of solid CDs materials limited. Apart from that, the widely reported CDs-based solid materials are blue or green-emitting. Long-wavelength emissive CDs-based materials, which are more appropriate as fluorescent labels in forensic science, information encrypting, and anti-counterfeiting, are still scarce. It is urgent to develop a facile and efficient strategy for the synthesis of solid CDs-based materials with long-wavelength emission.

To the best of our knowledge, the steady-state and time-resolved optical properties of CDs-based materials have been extensively studied [28–30]. However, their optical properties under variable temperature and pressure are rarely reported [9,31]. Most of the works have mainly emphasized on the synthesis and applications. Since the optical properties might be varied from case to case, it is necessary to explore the optical properties in detail.

Herein, a red-emissive phosphor based on CDs (SR-CDs) was facilely fabricated through an in-situ encapsulation method, with the assistance of zeolitic imidazolate frameworks (ZIF-8). Not only the fluorescence of CDs is preserved in solid state, but also the quantum yield is greatly improved to over 40 %. The optical properties were thoroughly explored by steady-state and time-resolved fluorescence measurement. Meanwhile, emissions under varying temperature and pressure were also monitored, which showed an increasing trend with the lowering of temperature and a decreasing trend with the increasing of pressure. By virtue of the excellent optical properties, the prepared material is potential in anti-counterfeiting, latent fingerprint imaging, and temperature/pressure-sensing field. This work opens up a promising way of preparing long-wavelength emitting carbon-based solid phosphors for special applications.
4-piperidinoaniline, 2-methylimidazole (Mim, 99 %), zinc nitrate hexahydrate (99 %) and other regents were commercially obtained and used without further purification.

The bulk phase purities were confirmed by powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) on a Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a Thermo Scientific Nicolet IS 50 FTIR spectrometer. The UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained on an Agilent Cary 5000 UV–vis-NIR spectrometer with an integrating sphere attachment. Photoluminescent properties were measured using an FLS980 fluorescence spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were examined on a Kratos AXIS Supra + spectrometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using a Tecnai G2 F20S electron microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the red emissive phosphor
ce:section.ce:para: The red emissive phosphor was prepared through an in-situ encapsulation strategy. Firstly, the red-emissive CDs (R-CDs) were synthesized according to a previous work [14]. Then 30 µL of R-CDs solution was added into 25 mL of methanol solution containing 12.0 mmol Mim. A methanol solution of 3.0 mmol Zn(NO3)2 was subsequently added into the above solution under vigorous stirring. Pale pink powder of SR-CDs was obtained by centrifuging and washed with methanol for three times. As a contrast, the corresponding ZIF-8 was prepared in a similar way without the addition of R-CDs.

The morphology of SR-CDs was investigated by SEM and TEM. SEM micrograph (Fig. 1
a) shows the material is orderly arranged and possesses a regular hexagonal prism morphology with the size around 200 nm. The TEM image (Fig. 1 b) also exhibits a clear hexagonal shape for the particles, similarly to the reported ZIF-8 [32,33]. The morphology of ZIF-8 has no significant change after the encapsulation of R-CDs, indicating the integrity of the zeolitic structure in the resultant SR-CDs. The morphology analysis of the pristine R-CDs was also carried out. The corresponding TEM image (Fig. 1 c) displays a monodispersed, spherical shape, with the particle size of no more than 5.0 nm. To obtain further insight into the structure of SR-CDs, PXRD measurement was performed. As can be seen in Fig. 1 d, the diffraction pattern of SR-CDs displays strong and sharp peaks, suggesting a good crystallinity. The PXRD diffraction peaks can be well matched with that of ZIF-8, indicating the resultant SR-CDs has the same zeolitic structure with that of ZIF-8. The characteristic diffraction signals emerge at about 7.4° (011), 10.4° (002), 12.7° (112), 14.6° (022), 16.4° (013), and 18.0° (222), which is coincident with that of ZIF-8 [34,35]. The result demonstrates that the resultant SR-CDs poss34ess the same structure with the corresponding ZIF-8, after the in-situ encapsulation of R-CDs.

The chemical composition of SR-CDs was examined by XPS and FTIR spectra. As shown in Fig. 2
a, the XPS spectrum reveals that the material contains four elements, that is, zinc, carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen. The elemental analysis was further performed and the results are C 64.50 %, O 6.64 %, N 17.18 %, and Zn 11.19 %. The FTIR spectrum of SR-CDs consists of typical absorptions, originated from R-CDs and ZIF-8. The characteristic absorption bands of O − H/N − H (∼3430 cm−1), C − H (2924 cm−1), C = O/N (∼1577 cm−1), C = C (∼1464 cm−1), and C − O/N (∼1142, 1022 cm−1) are observed in Fig. 2 b. The strong vibration peaks at 1350–1500 cm−1 and 600–1000 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching and bending vibration of imidazole ring, respectively. Besides, the characteristic absorption band at 420 cm−1 is also observed, which could be assigned to the Zn–N stretching vibration [36]. Zeta potential measurement shows that the prepared ZIF-8 is positively-charged (+6.27 mV), and the R-CDs nanoparticles are negatively-charged (−2.32 mV). Based on the above results, it can be deduced that SR-CDs is successfully assembled via electrostatic interaction, and maintains the native properties and structures of R-CDs and ZIF-8.

As shown in Fig. S1 , the as-prepared SR-CDs show a pale pink color under ambient condition, whereas a strong red emission is observed under UV illumination in the dark ( Fig. S2 ). The optical properties were explored by UV–vis and fluorescence spectra. In comparison with R-CDs and ZIF-8, the absorption spectrum of SR-CDs has an intense broad excitonic absorption peak centered at ∼ 500 nm, accompanied with two relatively small absorptions from 250 to 400 nm ( Fig. S3 ). The related emission spectra were recorded under different excitation wavelengths. As shown in Fig. 3
a, the main emission peaks are located at about 630 nm, with no significant shift observed in a wide excitation range. The color coordinates are varied from (0.42, 0.26) to (0.71, 0.30). However, they are all distributed in the red color range. The stability of SR-CDs was then studied under ambient condition (Fig. 3 b). The intensity slightly changes under continuous illumination of 3600 s, indicating a good photostability of SR-CDs. To gain further insight into exciton recombination dynamics, the time-resolved fluorescence spectrum was measured at the emission of 630 nm, excited by 330 nm (Fig. 3 c). The experimental curve shows a mono-exponential decay with a calculated fluorescence lifetime of 10.6 ns. The absolute quantum yield is measured to be ∼ 45 %, appreciably higher than that of 18 % for R-CDs. The enhanced quantum yield is probably ascribed to the assemble of R-CDs into the structure of ZIF-8, which prevents the aggregation of R-CDs nanoparticles and consequently suppresses non-radiative pathways.

To acquire more intrinsic characteristics of the photogenerated excitons in SR-CDs, temperature-dependent fluorescence spectra were also recorded in the range of 7 K to 290 K. As presented in Fig. 4
a, profiles of emission spectra are hardly changed with the decreasing of temperature, except for the enhanced emission intensities and slight band shifting. The emission peaks appear in the range of 620–630 nm, with full-width at half-maximum (FWHM) around 80 nm. The maximum band intensities show a dramatic increase at the initial cooling period and then a slower increase at the later cooling period. The variation of intensity with the temperature is plotted in Fig. 4 b. The temperature-evolution displays a sharp increase when the sample is cooled from 290 to 140 K, then the increase becomes slow, and finally almost reaches a plateau in the range of 60-7 K. The integrated intensity has been plotted as a function of inverse temperature based on the reported equation [31]:
I
T
=
I 0
1
+
A
e
-
E b
K B
T

where I(T) is referred to the integrated fluorescence intensity at the temperature of T, I 0 is the integrated intensity at 0 K, E b is referred to the exciton binding energy, and K B is the Boltzmann constant. The experimental results are in good agreement with the fitting curve based on the above equation (Fig. 4 c). Exciton binding energy of SR-CDs is calculated to be 110.6 ± 19.7 m eV, comparable to the reported value of CDs [9]. The corresponding chromaticity points in the CIE color plot (Fig. 4 d) are hardly shifted with the temperature, with the coordinates located at (0.63, 0.35). In order to get more insights into the variable-temperature fluorescence performance of SR-CDs, time-resolved fluorescence measurements were performed at variable temperatures (Fig. 4 e). It can be found that all the decay curves are well fitted with a mono-exponential function as below [37]: I(t) = A exp(t/τ), where I is the fluorescence intensity at time t, τ is the fluorescence lifetime, and A is a constant. As the time-resolved fluorescence spectra shown, the emission decays slowly with the decreasing of temperature. The values of fluorescence lifetime (Fig. 4 f) are measured to be 10–14 ns, first showing an increasing trend with the lowering of temperature, and then reaching a plateau. The phenomenon is consistent with that of emission intensity. The change of variable-temperature fluorescence decays and intensities could be attributed to the reduced nonradiative recombination at low temperature [31].

Pressure is confirmed to be an important parameter on the fluorescence intensity and wavelength [38–41]. The prepared SR-CDs powder was compressed into tablets under variable pressure. Comparable to the pristine powder, the corresponding fluorescence spectrum (Fig. 5
a) reveals that the emission wavelength remains unaltered at different pressure. The CIE coordinates are distributed in the red color range and slightly shifted (Fig. 5 b). Whereas, the intensity is greatly decreased with the increasing pressure. Pressure is considered to result in a close packing and reduce the interatomic or intermolecular distances. Therefore, when the external pressure was applied on SR-CDs, the distance between the fluorescent nanoparticles would decrease. In this way, the π-π stacking of nanoparticles would be induced, accompanied with the decrease of fluorescence intensity [39]. This phenomenon is very similar to that of carbon-based nanomaterials observed at high pressure [38]. Fig. 5 c provides the quantum yields of SR-CDs at different pressures, in which the highest quantum yield of 45 % is obtained at the lowest applied pressure. Time-resolved fluorescence spectra at variable pressure (Fig. 5 d) were measured, in order to explore the influence of pressure on the optical behavior of SR-CDs. The decay curves are identical to each other, and the calculated lifetime has no significant change. The contributions of radiative transition lifetimes (τr ) and nonradiative transition lifetimes (τnr ) could be obtained according to the following equations [37,42]: τ = φ × τr ;
1 / τ
=
1 /
τ r
+
1 /
τ
nr
. Here φ refers to the value of quantum yield and τ is the fluorescence lifetime. The decreased φ and almost unchanged τ with the increasing pressure suggest that the radiative recombination lifetime becomes much slower than the relative nonradiative recombination lifetime. Therefore, a reductive emission intensity appears at higher pressure. The results further confirm that R-CDs particles without protection of matrices lead to aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching at solid or aggregation state. Encapsulation of R-CDs into the porous ZIF-8 makes R-CDs particles separated from each other, thereby possessing an elevated fluorescence quantum yield and a stable red-emissive intensity in solid state, which is favorable for anti-counterfeiting, latent fingerprint imaging and so on.

In summary, we reported an extraordinary solid red emissive phosphor based on R-CDs through an in-situ encapsulation strategy with the assistance of ZIF-8. The obtained SR-CDs possess a higher red emission intensity in solid state, with CIE coordinate of (0.63, 0.35). It not only overcomes the disadvantage of aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching of R-CDs, but also successfully obtains enhanced emission intensity and quantum yield in solid state. Fluorescence properties were explored in detail, and it is found that the emission intensity is responsive to temperature and applied pressure. Based on the excellent emission performance, this material has great potential for applications in anti-counterfeiting, latent fingerprint imaging, and temperature/pressure-sensing. This work provides a promising and facile way of preparing carbon-based solid phosphors for special applications.

Wen Wang: Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Jinbao Wei: Investigation, Formal analysis. Yutong Ding: Formal analysis. Yike Yang: Formal analysis. Xin Zhou: Formal analysis. Hong-min Meng: Writing – review & editing. Qianqian Zhu: Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Zhaohui Li: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Humanities and Social Sciences Youth Foundation of Ministry of Education of the People’s Republic of China (21YJC820056), Key Research & Development program in Henan Province (232102230002), and the Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program for College Students in Henan Province (202210459171).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.124235.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
